narrative_blocks.header: On the morning his mother had her stroke
narrative_blocks.body: May 17, 1909: On the same morning that First Lady Nellie Taft suffered her severe stroke on the presidential yacht, eleven-year-old Charles Phelps Taft II was undergoing what his biographers noted as a 'bloody adenoid operation.' The president's household absorbed two medical crises simultaneously. Charles was not told about his mother's stroke until he recovered.

narrative_blocks.header: Fortune called his Cincinnati the best-managed big city
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Phelps Taft II served as Mayor of Cincinnati from 1955 to 1957, during which Fortune magazine ranked Cincinnati the best-managed large city in the United States. He was known as 'Mr. Cincinnati.' He was also known for keeping a canoe tied to his car at all times, in anticipation of his next trip. When he died in 1983, his family had 'Gone fishing' inscribed on his grave at Spring Grove Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his brother Robert founded the law
narrative_blocks.body: In 1924, Charles Phelps Taft II and his brother Robert A. Taft co-founded Taft Stettinius & Hollister in Cincinnati — a firm that continues to carry the Taft name. He had been youngest-ever president of the International YMCA, served as Hamilton County Prosecutor, and helped introduce the city manager form of government that Cincinnati — the first major city to adopt it — still uses today.

narrative_blocks.header: He ran for Mayor of Cleveland in 1967
narrative_blocks.body: Seth Chase Taft ran for Mayor of Cleveland in 1967 against Carl B. Stokes — and lost. Stokes became the first African American mayor of a major U.S. city.

notable.notable_blurb: Economics professor; Yale PhD despite polio paralysis

narrative_blocks.header: Polio at fourteen; a Yale PhD anyway
narrative_blocks.body: Paralyzed by polio in 1942, she went on to a Vassar B.A., a London School of Economics M.A., and a Yale doctorate, then taught economics at American University and Smith. Her grandfather had been President of the United States.

cross_connections.display_label: taught history and economics at Northern Iowa in her era

narrative_blocks.header: She died of polio in 1941
narrative_blocks.body: Rosalyn Rawson Taft died in 1941 at approximately age 11. Her sister Cynthia Herron Taft contracted polio the following year, 1942, and was paralyzed from the waist down. The two sisters and a single disease — one killed, one paralyzed — one year apart.

political_offices.title: Chief Justice of the United States
political_offices.notes: Only person to serve as both President and Chief Justice

HD0338.prominent_name_reason: 27th President of the United States (1909-1913) and Chief Justice of the Supreme Court (1921-1930). 7th-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker through the line: Thomas Hooker (H00001) → Sarah Hooker Wilson (H00006) → Susanna Wilson Rawson (H00026) → Rev. Grindal Rawson (I00005, father) × Susanna Wilson → John Rawson (H00080) → Rhoda Rawson Taft (H00215) → Peter Rawson Taft (HD0336) → Alphonso Taft (HD0337) → William Howard Taft.

notable.notable_blurb: 27th President and 10th Chief Justice of the

cross_connections.display_label: Munson served as his Manila health officer in 1903 — two Hooker descendants, one running the Philippines.
His father Alphonso co-founded Skull and Bones in 1832 with this man's great-uncle — he joined the same society forty-four years later.

burial.plot_notes: First president and first Supreme Court justice buried at Arlington

narrative_blocks.header: He turned down the Supreme Court three times
narrative_blocks.body: Theodore Roosevelt offered William Howard Taft a seat on the Supreme Court in 1902, again in 1906, and again in 1906. Each time, Taft refused — he felt his work in the Philippines or the War Department wasn't finished. The presidency he ran for in 1908 was, in his own words, 'awful to be afraid of.' The Chief Justiceship — appointed by Harding in 1921 — was what he had always wanted.

narrative_blocks.header: Two months into his presidency
narrative_blocks.body: On May 17, 1909 — just ten weeks after his inauguration — Nellie Taft suffered a severe stroke that left her partially paralyzed and unable to speak. Taft spent hours each day working with her, teaching her to speak again. It took a year.

narrative_blocks.header: Nellie had visited the White House at 17
narrative_blocks.body: Helen Herron, age 17, visited the White House as a guest of President and Mrs. Hayes — intimate friends of her parents. The visit fixed an ambition. Years later, when her husband was repeatedly offered the Supreme Court seat he actually wanted, it was Nellie who pushed back toward the presidency.

narrative_blocks.header: Humorists suggested 'TAFT' stood for 'Take Advice
narrative_blocks.body: Theodore Roosevelt was so involved in Taft's 1908 campaign — pressuring delegates, issuing presidential statements, shaping the platform — that humorists coined the acronym 'TAFT': Take Advice from Theodore. Taft himself had said he needed Roosevelt's 'judgment and criticism' for his acceptance speech. Roosevelt later deeply regretted his role in making Taft president and ran against him in 1912, splitting the Republican vote and handing the presidency to Wilson.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Rawson cousins each married a Taft
narrative_blocks.body: His Hooker ancestry arrived through two independent marriages. Mary Dorr Taft (gen 4, Hooker/Rawson descendant) married Rev. Moses Taft — uncle of Aaron Taft, who in the same generation married Rhoda Rawson Taft (also a Hooker/Rawson descendant, gen 5). Mary and Rhoda were first cousins once removed.

institutions.institution_blurb: Accepted his 1908 presidential nomination from this portico.

burial.plot_notes: Buried beside her husband at Arlington National Cemetery

sources.label: White House Historical Association — Helen Taft

notable.notable_blurb: First Lady who planted D.C.'s cherry blossoms

narrative_blocks.header: She visited the White House at 17
narrative_blocks.body: Helen Herron visited the White House at age 17 as the guest of President Hayes, an intimate friend of her parents. The visit set an ambition. When her husband was repeatedly offered the Supreme Court seat he actually wanted more than the presidency, it was Nellie who pushed him toward Washington.

narrative_blocks.header: She ordered the cherry trees planted around
narrative_blocks.body: Nellie Taft requested the Japanese cherry trees that were planted along the Tidal Basin in 1912 — they are among Washington's most lasting landmarks. She did not get to enjoy them fully: on May 17, 1909, just ten weeks after the inauguration, she suffered a severe stroke on the presidential yacht. Taft spent hours each day teaching her to speak again.

narrative_blocks.header: He co-founded Skull and Bones
narrative_blocks.body: Alphonso Taft co-founded Skull and Bones at Yale in 1832 with William Huntington Russell — who was also a Hooker descendant, though neither man knew it. His son William Howard Taft joined the same society, as did Henry Waters Taft and Horace Dutton Taft. Alphonso served as Attorney General, Secretary of War, and U.S. Minister to both Austria-Hungary and Russia — but his greatest legacy was the political dynasty: William became the only person to hold both the presidency and the Chief Justiceship.

narrative_blocks.header: He dissented in the Bible-in-schools case
narrative_blocks.body: In 1872, Alphonso Taft dissented from his own court's ruling requiring Bible reading in Cincinnati's public schools. He argued that the government must be neutral toward all religions, referencing Jewish taxpayers' right to secular education. The opinion was widely admired but killed his 1875 bid for the Republican nomination for Governor of Ohio.

narrative_blocks.header: His father Peter Rawson honored Hooker ancestry
narrative_blocks.body: His father was Peter Rawson Taft — the middle name "Rawson" an explicit tribute to Peter's mother Rhoda Rawson Taft, who descended from Rev. Grindal Rawson and Susanna Wilson Rawson, herself a daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker's daughter Sarah Hooker Wilson. The name traveled three generations to Alphonso's father before reaching him.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-founded Skull and Bones at Yale in 1832 — both were Thomas Hooker descendants, though neither knew it.
Ohio Republican Party founding-era figures — lawyer-jurist and newspaper editor.

education.notes: — Co-founder of Skull and Bones 1832 with William Huntington Russell. Father of President William Howard Taft. Hooker descendant.

notable.notable_blurb: Yale satirist; mother of President Taft

cross_connections.display_label: Published The Yale Gallinipper together 1846-1858 — a satirical Yale newspaper written anonymously, pretending to be 'three brothers' who were undergraduates.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve years writing as “three brothers”
narrative_blocks.body: With Olivia Day and Henrietta Blake she published The Yale Gallinipper anonymously from 1846 to 1858, the trio signing themselves “three brothers.” She married Alphonso Taft — her fourth cousin twice removed — and raised a president.

narrative_blocks.header: A fourth cousin, twice removed
narrative_blocks.body: She married Alphonso Taft — her fourth cousin twice removed — the day after Christmas 1853, becoming stepmother to two sons and, in time, mother of a President. Mount Holyoke, class of 1845.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
